content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LA\)U
555 CAPITOL MALL, SUITE 625
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 9581 4
9t61264-1818
FAx9161264-t824
October 6, L992
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
Fair Political Practices commission
P.O. Box 8O7
Sacranento, cA 95804-0807
Dear Ur. Hal labrin:
This is a request for informal assistance pursuant to
covernment code section 83114(b). we are requesting this
assistance on behalf of current and future clients of this law
firm.
FACIS
An elected state officer who runs for a 1oca1 office in a
jurisdiction irhich has adopted a campaign ordinance is required by
the Political Reforn Act and by the local ordinance to establish a
separate conmittee and campaign bank account for the loca1
election. 
A11 contributi.ons received for the local election must
be deposited into that account and aII expenditures made to support
the local candidacy must be nade from that account.
In addition, the Local campaign ordinance imposes a strict
prohibition on transfers of contributions from one candidate's
funds to another's, and provides that:
contributions raised for one city office nay
only be used for that city office and for no
other campaign.
QUESTIOII
If an elected state officer's committee for a local election
is prohibited from spending any of its funds to benefit the
officeholder's state candidacy, is the local cornmittee required to
file carnpaign statenents on the state filing 
schedule?
DISCUSSIOI{
Previously, FPPC staff concluded in the Barrett Advice Letter,
No, A-88-15o, and in the Fishburn Advice Letter, No. I-91-325, that
when an elected state officer runs for a local office for which the
.€"


\' 
;.


scott Hallabrin
Page Thro
election is held on a date different from the state election,
original campaign statements of the local commj.ttee must be filed
at the sane tines and in the same places as the statements required
to be filed by the state connittee. Presumably this would include
pre-election statements and late contribution reports.
The analysis provided in the Barrett Advice Letter, states
that, lrith regard to candidate-controll-ed general purpose
committees:
one reason for this advice is that such comnittees
are not prohibited from rnaking expenditures in
support of the controlling officeholder or candidate
and, therefore, it is iruportant to also have the
rrgeneral purposert committee's canpaign statenents
available in a tinely manner to the voters in the
jurlsdiction of the officeholder or candidate,
particularly when he or she is being voted upon....
Barrett Advice Letter, Page Tvro
we believe the reason for this requirement as set out in the
Barrett Advice Letter is sound as it applies to controlled
comnittees which may make expenditures to support the controLling
candidate or officeholder. It is iloportant for the voters to have
access to all infornation concerning carnpaign activity which night
have an influence on the election.
However, the conclusion in the previous advice letters does
not take into consideration the situation in which a local
conmittee is strictly prohibited from roaking any expenditures to
benefit the state candidacy.
For example, candidates for Los Angeles city elections for
llayor, City Attorney, controller and City Council are governed by
carnpaign fj-nance provisions which are sinilar to those previously
irnposed by state Proposition 73. (charter Amendnents, enacted in
1985 and 1987, ueasure H, enacted in June, 1990 and City Council
ordinance adopted in 1990.) The city charter i-nposes a strict
prohibition on transfers of contributions fron one candidate's
funds to another's, and provides that:
Contributions raised for one city office nay
only be used for that city office and for no
other campaign.
(Los Angeles city
charter section 3L2.c.1-1. )


Scott Hallabrin
Page Three
In light of this strict prohibition on the use of funds held
by a city candidate's committee, there is no public purpose served
in requiring that the city committee file carnpaign statements on
the state election schedule.
In addition, there are two very significant reasons why the
local conmittee should not bs required to file canpaign statenents
on the state election schedule:
First, the requirernent to file 
statements on the state
election schedul-e inposes a tremendous burden and expense on the
state officeholder's loca1 comnittee which are not irnposed on any
of the other candidates or conmittees in the local election. Most
significantly, the requirement to file telegrarns with four state
fiJ.ing officers within 24 hours of receiving $1,0o0 from a single
source during the late contribution period for the state election
results in a great burden and expense not incurred by the other
local candidates and conmittees.
second, rather than providing meaningful infornation to the
voters, the filing of late contribution reports by the local
committee with the state filing officers nay actually cause
confusion to the voters. The 1ocal conmittee is prohibited frorn
using any of its funds in connecti.on with the state election and,
therefore, contributions received by the local corunittee during
the 16 days before the state election are DqE trlate contributions. rl
There is no purpose served in requiring their fiting and, in fact,
the filing of the late reports would be misleading to the voters.
In addition, there is the issue of even-handedness. None of
the other candidates, committees, or contributors involved in the
Iocal election are required to file statements on those odd dates
(in most cases, three nonths earlier than the serni-annual
statements required of a1l cormittees). And, because the noney
received by the local cotrmittee nay not be used to benefit the
controlling candidate's election to state office, the burden and
expense are not justified.
SUI'IIARY
In light of the fact that the candidate-controlled coromittee
which is primarily forned for a local election is strictly
prohibited fron using any of its funds for any purpose other than
the Local election, no public purpose is served by the early filing
of the local connittee's campaign staternents with state filing
officers.


Scott Hallabrin
Page Four
we would greatly appreciate your consideration of this issue
as qulckly as possibte, as late contribution reports nust be filed
beglnning on october 18.
P1ea6e caII ne if you have any questions or would like any
additignal inf ornatlon.
di;^ ?*h

sections.question: If an elected state officer's committee for a local election is prohibited from spending any of its funds to benefit the officeholder's state candidacy, is the local committee required to file campaign statements on the state filing schedule?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether a local campaign committee for an elected state officer running for local office, which is strictly prohibited from using funds for the officer's state candidacy, must file campaign statements according to the state election schedule.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm seeking informal assistance under Government Code section 83114(b), not an FPPC response. It contains a clearly stated QUESTION section and detailed FACTS and DISCUSSION sections presenting the firm's arguments, but no CONCLUSION from FPPC. The document is dated October 6, 1992, and references prior FPPC advice letters (Barrett A-88-150 and Fishburn I-91-325).

embedding.summary: Law firm Remcho, Johansen & Purcell requests informal FPPC assistance regarding whether a local campaign committee of an elected state officer, strictly prohibited from spending funds on state elections, must file campaign statements on the state filing schedule. The firm argues that previous FPPC advice letters do not account for this situation and contends that such filing creates burden and confusion without public benefit.
